title: Screen Time Use Among US Adolescents During the COVID-19 Pandemic: Findings From the Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development (ABCD) Study.
abstract: This cross-sectional study reviews findings from the Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development (ABCD) Study on digital media use by US youths during the COVID-19 pandemic.

disagreed_criteria.c1_evidence: Paper does not explicitly state ABCD study membership in title or abstract. Text indicates data are from 'May 2020 COVID-19 Rapid Response Research Release' and references the ABCD Study, but the letter format and phrasing ('This cross-sectional study reviews findings from...') suggests secondary analysis of ABCD data, not primary ABCD participation by these authors. The study is published as a Letter in JAMA Pediatrics, not a full ABCD study report. Per FT-IC1, must be original ABCD analysis or clear US ABCD sub-sample work; this reads as a selective reporting letter rather than an ABCD consortium or ABCD-participating team analysis.
disagreed_criteria.c2_evidence: ABCD Study, n=5412 US adolescents aged 10-14, May 2020 COVID-19 survey

disagreed_criteria.c1_evidence: Paper is a Letter (not full research article) and explicitly states 'This cross-sectional study reviews findings from the Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development (ABCD) Study.' This is secondary analysis/reporting of ABCD data, not original ABCD empirical analysis by ABCD team. Per B14, letter/review format combined with secondary reporting fails FT-IC5.
disagreed_criteria.c2_evidence: Original empirical analysis of ABCD data with SM→outcome relationships examined

title: Associations of Screen Time and Physical Activity With Body Mass Index in Early Adolescence: A Prospective Cohort Study.
abstract: OBJECTIVE: This study aimed toÂ prospectively identify the independent, mutually adjusted, and interactive associations of screen time and physical activity with BMI and overweight/obesity risk in adolescents. METHODS: This study analyzed prospective data from 5356âUS adolescents in the Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development (ABCD) Study, Year 2 (2018-2020, ages 11-12) and Year 4 (2020-2022, ages 13-14). RESULTS: Mean daily screen time was 6.1 (Â±5.2) hours and mean step count was 9265 (Â±3227) steps in Year 2; 32.7% were classified with overweight/obesity in Year 4. In Poisson regression models examining the exposures mutually adjusted for each other, high screen time (>â8âh/day) was associated with higher risk of overweight/obesity (risk ratio [RR], 1.09; 95% CI, 1.02-1.16; pâ=â0.013) compared with low screen time (0-4âh/day). In multivariable linear and Poisson regression models, respectively, low step count (1000-6000 steps/day) was associated with higher BMI percentile (coefficient [B], 3.27; 95% CI, 1.54-4.99; pâ<â0.001) and higher risk of overweight/obesity (RR, 1.23; 95% CI, 1.07-1.40; pâ=â0.003) compared with high step count (>â12,000 steps/day). No significant interactions were observed between screen time and step count. CONCLUSIONS: These findings suggest that obesity prevention efforts should consider both greater physical activity and less recreational screen use as behavioral interventions.

disagreed_criteria.c1_evidence: Self-reported recreational screen time (comprehensive measure: TV, videos, games, texting, SM, video chat) measured via ABCD Youth Screen Time Survey. Qualifies as time on screen modality per FT-IC2; covers smartphones/SM per B1.
disagreed_criteria.c2_evidence: Screen time only (no per-item breakdown of platform/app/content). Reports total daily screen time (6.1±5.2 h/day) without distinguishing smartphone, tablet, TV, or gaming platform. B2 applies: summed/total screen time with no per-item breakdown.

title: Childhood internalizing, externalizing and attention symptoms predict changes in social and nonsocial screen time.
abstract: BACKGROUND: While accumulating research has tested the hypothesis that screen time causes psychiatric symptoms in children, less attention has been paid to the hypothesis that children with psychiatric symptoms change their patterns of screen time and digital media use. We aimed to test whether children with psychiatric symptoms subsequently change their patterns of screen time and digital media use. METHODS: Nâ=â9,066 children primarily aged 9-10 in the Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development Study at baseline and 1-year later. Psychiatric symptoms included internalizing, attention, and externalizing symptoms. Screen time was measured as ordinally defined weekday and weekend time on social and nonsocial [e.g., YouTube] digital media). Models assessed psychiatric symptoms as predictors of screen time, and screen time as predictors of psychiatric symptoms, controlled for baseline measures of each, sex, age, race/ethnicity, and income. RESULTS: Children with psychiatric symptoms spent more time on non-social media one year later compared with peers. Considering total psychiatric problems, clinical levels of problems predicted higher levels of weekday (ORâ=â1.22, 95% CI 1.22-1.23) and weekend (ORâ=â1.10, 95% CI 1.09-1.11) nonsocial screen time. For nearly all analyses of psychiatric symptoms predicting screen time, associations were highest for a non-social screen time outcome rather than a social screen time outcome (Highest ORâ=â1.65, 95% CI 1.63-1.67, clinical rule breaking predicting weekday nonsocial screen time). Comparable magnitude associations were observed for social and nonsocial media use predicting future psychiatric symptoms, suggesting bidirectionality. CONCLUSION: Children with psychiatric symptoms have different subsequent media use patterns, including higher rates of subsequent nonsocial engagement. Ensuring that ongoing data collection and analysis efforts attend to temporality and transitions in the relation between media use and psychiatric symptoms will accelerate progress in the field.

disagreed_criteria.c1_evidence: FAILS condition (a): Paper has BOTH directions - psychiatric symptoms → screen time AND screen time → psychiatric symptoms. Models assess psychiatric symptoms as predictors of screen time AND screen time as predictors of psychiatric symptoms. Applies B18: bidirectional analysis disqualifies FT-EC7.
disagreed_criteria.c2_evidence: BINDING RULE B20: Paper tests bidirectional/cross-lagged associations. Abstract states 'Comparable magnitude associations were observed for social and nonsocial media use predicting future psychiatric symptoms, suggesting bidirectionality.' Models include both psychiatric symptoms→screen use AND screen use→psychiatric symptoms pathways. FT-EC7 disqualifies bidirectional analyses per binding rule.
